ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for agedashi tofu (deep-fried tofu in dashi broth). FOODON_03302050 (tofu food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Tofu) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for agedashi tofu as a prepared dish with dashi broth and katakuriko coating.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for agedashi tofu or sufficiently close prepared tofu dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of agedashi tofu is approximately 200 g, consisting of 3–4 pieces of deep-fried silken tofu in a warm tsuyu dashi broth, garnished with grated daikon, green onion, and bonito flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Agedashi Tofu — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Tofu, soft (FDC ID 172443) as base, adjusted for deep-frying in vegetable oil and dashi broth components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for agedashi tofu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA FDC soft tofu (FDC 172443) as the primary base (~60% by weight), with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10% fat uptake per 100 g fried product), dashi broth contribution (low calorie, sodium ~200 mg/100 mL), and katakuriko starch coating (~5 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are cross-validated against published Japanese food composition data (Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan, 2020, item 04032 Tofu, deep-fried, and item 17026 Mentsuyu/tsuyu sauce).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean products and tofu — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based foods; GI of plain tofu reported as approximately 15–35 (low); agedashi tofu estimated at GI 35 due to starch coating and broth sugars
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for agedashi tofu. The University of Sydney GI Database reports plain tofu as low GI (approximately 15). Agedashi tofu is estimated at GI 35 (low) to account for the katakuriko (potato starch) coating and small amount of mirin/sugar in the tsuyu broth, which modestly elevate the GI above plain tofu. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.6) / 100 ≈ 5.5, using 200 g serving with 7.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Agedashi tofu contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (560 mg per standard 200 g serving) primarily from the tsuyu dashi broth and soy sauce; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as the sodium contribution can be significant relative to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Agedashi tofu has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.5 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high soy protein content may also support glycaemic control, though the starch coating and mirin in the broth contribute modest carbohydrates that should be accounted for in meal planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy isoflavones, unsaturated fat profile, and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Agedashi tofu provides soy isoflavones and a predominantly unsaturated fat profile (polyunsaturated fat ~3.6 g per 100 g, saturated fat ~0.9 g per 100 g), which are broadly cardioprotective; however, the sodium content from the dashi broth warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are managing blood pressure, and deep-frying adds total fat that should be considered in overall dietary fat targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Agedashi tofu (揚げ出し豆腐, agedashi dōfu) is a classic Japanese dish consisting of silken or soft tofu that is lightly coated in potato starch (katakuriko), deep-fried until the exterior develops a delicate, lightly crisp golden crust, and then served in a warm, savoury tsuyu broth made from dashi (typically kombu and bonito flakes), soy sauce, and mirin. The dish is traditionally garnished with finely grated daikon radish, sliced green onion, and dried bonito flakes (katsuobushi). The texture contrast between the silky, custardy interior of the tofu and the thin, slightly crisp starch coating that softens as it absorbs the broth is a defining characteristic of the dish. Agedashi tofu is a staple of Japanese izakaya (gastropub) menus and is also served in traditional kaiseki and teishoku (set meal) contexts. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-calorie dish providing plant-based protein from soy (~5.8 g per 100 g), predominantly unsaturated fats from the frying oil, and modest carbohydrates from the starch coating and broth. The sodium content is notable due to the soy sauce and dashi broth, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated as low (approximately 35), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions. Soy isoflavones present in the tofu base may offer additional cardiovascular and hormonal health benefits. In Singapore, agedashi tofu is widely available at Japanese restaurants and izakaya-style dining establishments.
common_user_queries: Is agedashi tofu suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in agedashi tofu?
Is agedashi tofu high in sodium?
What is the GI of agedashi tofu?
What does agedashi tofu taste like?
What is the difference between agedashi tofu and regular fried tofu?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for agedashi tofu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FDC soft tofu (FDC 172443) as the primary base, cross-validated against the Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020 (items 04032 and 17026). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain tofu as the closest reference food, adjusted upward for starch coating and broth sugars. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, multi-source, and cross-validated.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated; no direct agedashi tofu entry was found in any approved primary source. Values derived from USDA FDC soft tofu (FDC ID 172443) adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and dashi broth sodium, cross-validated against Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the source databases used. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable frying oils used in Japanese cooking contain negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FDC reference data. The food_id GD004178 and restaurant attribution (GyoGyo - Funan, field survey data) are preserved from the input seed record. The menu label '703D Agedashi Tofu(D)' is retained as an alias; the canonical food name is normalised to 'Agedashi Tofu'. Halal status is not confirmed as dashi typically contains bonito (fish); is_halal is set to false by default pending restaurant-specific confirmation. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for plain tofu, adjusted for katakuriko starch coating and mirin content in broth.
